10.1016/j.jmp.2021.102521
Organisms continuously receive information from the environment through multiple sensory organs. It is crucial for organisms, including humans, to process multisensory information efficiently. A growing body of literature has demonstrated that multisensory integration can be viewed as optimal Bayesian integration (e.g., ( Alais & Burr, 2004; Ernst & Banks, 2002; Trommershäuser et al., 2011 ); see ( Colonius & Diederich, 2018 ) for a recent review). These studies have demonstrated that information from different modalities are combined in a statistically optimal manner.

However, combining information from different modalities is not always beneficial if different types of information do not share the same source and do not pertain to the same event. An observer’s belief regarding multisensory stimuli belonging to the same event is referred to as the ‘unity assumption’, which is considered an important factor in multisensory integration ( Chen & Spence, 2017; Welch & Warren, 1980 ) for a review). In recent years, various Bayesian models that consider the causal relationships of multisensory stimuli, and combine stimuli only when they are causally related, have been proposed ( Körding et al., 2007; Sato et al., 2007 ). Such Bayesian causal inference models have been verified empirically, particularly in audio-visual integration, and spatial and temporal tasks ( Beierholm et al., 2009; Locke & Landy, 2017; Rohe & Noppeney, 2015b; Wozny et al., 2010 ). Recently, the neural basis for multisensory causal inference has also been explored using functional magnetic resonance imaging ( Cao et al., 2019; Rohe & Noppeney, 2015a ) and electroencephalography ( Rohe et al., 2019 ), as well as computational methods ( Cuppini et al., 2017; Ma & Rahmati, 2013; Yamashita et al., 2013 ). However, in some studies, particularly those on visual–vestibular integration tasks, it has been suggested that causal inference models do not always hold and that some multisensory stimuli are forcibly integrated (see not only  Acerbi et al., 2018; De Winkel et al., 2015 ; but also  De Winkel et al., 2017 ). Therefore, it is still unclear if Bayesian causal inference is a general computational principle or if it simply represents a limited range of multisensory integration.

Most of theoretical and empirical Bayesian causal inference studies have focused on the binary judgement of a pair of multisensory stimuli or sequences of stimuli, such as judging if a presented auditory and visual stimuli pair is simultaneous or which was presented first (notable exceptions are flash illusion studies that focus on the number of stimuli perceived; ( Shams et al., 2000, 2005 ). When there is only one pair of audio-visual stimuli, the causal structure is either the stimuli sharing the same source or not. Forced fusion can be interpreted as a special form of Bayesian causal inference in which the probability of the stimuli sharing the same source is equal to one. However, real-world environments are full of different events, and we frequently receive multiple stimuli simultaneously. When multiple events occur, the causal relationships between stimuli become more complex.

An empirical study by Roseboom et al. investigated a more complex scenarios ( Roseboom et al., 2009 ) for audio-visual integration tasks. In one task, participants were asked to judge the simultaneity of a pair of auditory (change in the frequency of a persistent tone) and visual (bounce of a moving disc)​ stimuli with various auditory stimulus timings relative to the visual stimulus. The stimuli were judged as simultaneous if the auditory stimulus was presented within a certain time range relative to the visual stimulus. This temporal range was referred to as the ‘temporal window of simultaneity’. In another task, an additional visual stimulus (bounce of another moving disc) was presented with the auditory stimulus and original visual stimulus (standard visual stimulus) at a time 100 ms before the standard visual stimulus in one condition. This additional visual stimulus ‘shifted’ the temporal window of simultaneity between the auditory and standard visual stimuli in the opposite direction of the additional stimulus, meaning the auditory stimulus had to be presented well after the standard visual stimulus to be judged as simultaneous with the standard stimulus. In the second experiment, the authors also demonstrated that the additional visual stimulus enhanced the sensitivity of temporal judgement between audio-visual stimuli.

Assuming that an auditory stimulus and two visual stimuli are clearly detectable and distinguishable, different causal structures are feasible. For example, the auditory stimulus may share the source of one of the visual stimuli or neither visual stimuli. According to the concept of forced fusion, it is also possible that the auditory stimulus is forcibly integrated with one of the visual stimuli, or that all three stimuli are integrated. However, the strategy that the human brain employs in such situations is unclear.

In this study, first, I propose three Bayesian models with different assumptions regarding causal structures for when an auditory stimulus can be combined with two possible visual stimuli, while keeping the models as minimum as possible. I show that the model that considers the full causal structure optimally explains existing experimental results better than the other two models. Moreover, I demonstrate that, by introducing another modelling parameter for simultaneity judgement criterion in different experimental conditions, the extended versions of the other two models also explain the experimental results. Additionally, I reveal that all three models are capable of explaining the enhancement of sensitivity to some degree. Although these models exhibit similar behaviours and are indistinguishable in the simulated experimental settings, they provide different interpretations regarding the underlying mechanism of the shift in the temporal window. Finally, I provide suggestions regarding experimental manipulations that can distinguish the three models.

In this study, the same experimental conditions as those in Experiment 1 presented by Roseboom et al. ( Roseboom et al., 2009 ) were considered ( Fig. 1 ). In one condition (baseline condition), a pair of visual and auditory stimuli was presented with various temporal delays, and subjects judged whether or not they perceived the audio-visual stimuli as simultaneous. In another condition (called the test condition in their study, but called the AV2 condition in this study for better clarity), two visual stimuli were presented at different locations (above or below fixation): one was a standard visual stimulus defining

The task of the subject in the AV2 condition was a four-alternative forced choice (4AFC) task with the following choices: the auditory stimulus was perceived as simultaneous with the visual stimulus above fixation, with the visual stimulus below fixation, with both visual stimuli, or with neither visual stimulus. For analysis, these 4AFC choices were translated into simultaneous with the standard visual stimulus, with the additional visual stimulus, with both stimuli, or with neither stimulus, respectively. These three conditions (baseline and two additional visual stimulus timing conditions) were randomly interspersed during trials.

The estimated timing for each stimulus can be determined based on the posterior distribution by either maximizing it, calculating its average, or sampling it. There is an ongoing debate regarding which strategy the brain follows ( Rohe & Noppeney, 2015b; Wozny et al., 2010 ). Overall, the averaging strategy seems to fit the experimental results for many tasks. Because the objective of this study was to compare different assumptions regarding causal structures, the averaging strategy was adopted for simplicity. Simultaneity between two stimuli is determined by whether or not the estimated timing of stimuli falls into a ‘temporal window of simultaneity’. Therefore, if

It must be noted that this criterion is not necessarily equivalent to the experimentally observed temporal window of simultaneity. When an auditory stimulus is integrated with a visual stimulus, their estimated timings are attracted to each other. Because simultaneity is judged based on the attracted estimated timings, stimuli with a relatively large physical temporal lag can still fall into a narrow criterion of simultaneity judgement.

The main focus in this study was to examine how differences in assumptions regarding the causal structure of events affect model predictions.

It is widely known that a pair of auditory and visual stimuli is most likely to be judged as simultaneous when the auditory stimulus lags slightly behind the visual stimulus (e.g., ( Dixon & Spitz, 1980; J.V. et al., 2001; Zampini et al., 2005 )). This may reflect faster auditory information processing compared to visual information processing ( Stein & Meredith, 1993 ), or compensation for the slower transduction time of sound ( Sugita & Suzuki, 2003 ); also see ( Arnold et al., 2005 ). The former effect can be modelled naturally as a temporal lag in the encoding stage, while the latter can be modelled at inference time. In this study, this effect was incorporated by introducing a  bias  parameter in the encoding (observation generation) stage (see Section  3.2  for more details), although model prediction does not change significantly when the bias parameter is incorporated at the inference stage for the situations considered in this study.

In this study, three possible causal structures for audio-visual stimuli in the AV2 condition are considered. In the first model, assuming that the auditory and visual stimuli are well above the detection threshold and clearly distinguishable, the auditory stimulus is integrated with at most one visual stimulus. An auditory stimulus can be integrated with either a standard visual stimulus, or additional visual stimulus, or neither stimuli. This first model is referred to as the ‘full causal model’. During model derivation, I propose a hierarchical formulation of causal structural priors in complex situations with multiple number of audio-visual stimuli. The second model, which is referred to as the ‘selective forced integration model’, is a forced integration model wherein the auditory stimulus is forcibly integrated with only one visual stimulus. The third model, which is referred to as the ‘full forced integration model’, is another form of forced integration model wherein the auditory stimulus is forcibly integrated with both visual stimuli. A baseline simplest model, which is referred to as the ‘no-binding model’, is also examined, in which all stimuli are independent and no binding occurs. At first, each model will be defined with as few parameters as possible, and then will be extended if necessary to retain the simplicity of the models, and enable the analysis of modelling factors that affect the qualitative prediction of the models.

In the full causal model, the causal structure that produced the auditory and visual stimuli are explicitly considered during inference. A variable  C  is introduced to represent the causal structure of the auditory and visual stimuli. The causal structure

It should be emphasized that the inference on the causal relationship is not equivalent to the judgement of simultaneity. For example, when an auditory and a visual stimulus are presented at completely different locations, or when they deliver inconsistent semantic content (e.g. a glass shuttering sound as an auditory stimulus and a barking dog as a visual stimulus), they are very unlikely to come from the same source. Nevertheless, if they are presented simultaneously, their timing can be judged as simultaneous with high probability ( Zampini et al., 2005 ).

In the case when there is one pair of audio-visual stimuli, possible causal structure  
                               C 
                             is simple: whether they came from the same event source or two separate sources ( Körding et al., 2007; Sato et al., 2007 ). However, in a more complex situation where there exist multiple auditory and visual stimuli, and hence, there are multiple possible combinations of audio-visual stimuli, the mathematical formulation of the causal structure is not straightforward. Furthermore, baseline and AV2 conditions were interspersed in the experiment by Roseboom et al. and the number of observed stimuli was also changed trial by trial. Therefore, the prior for the causal structures of event sources should also be modelled concurrently for different observed number of stimuli.

I formulate the prior distribution of the causal structure with the following assumptions related to the experimental settings to avoid making it complex. 1. All stimuli are clearly detected and distinguished. 2. All stimuli within each modality have the same properties other than timing; hence, there is no reason for an auditory stimulus to be bound to a specific visual stimulus a priori. 3. Each event source is a brief singular event in time and emits one visual and one auditory stimulus at most; hence, distinguished unimodal stimuli are not bound and only cross-modal bindings can occur. Without this assumption, it naturally extends to the assumption that an event source can emit any number of stimuli, which makes the model too complicated and intractable. For example, let us assume that two auditory and two visual stimuli are detected. If an event source can emit multiple auditory and/or visual stimuli, the possible number of event sources,  n , can range from 1 to 4. In the case where  n  
                            
                               = 
                             1, all four stimuli are emitted from it. In the case  n  
                            
                               = 
                             2, the number of possible causal structures is 2 4  
                            
                               − 
                             2 (the last term excludes the case where all stimuli are from one source). In the case  n  
                            
                               = 
                             3, the number of possible causal structures is 3 4  minus some exclusion terms, and so on. In this case, the number of possible causal structures is 53, whereas it is 7 with the assumption 3. For the case with three auditory and two visual stimuli, the number of possible causal structures is 543 without the assumption and 13 with the assumption. Thus, the model becomes intractable quickly even in relatively simple experimental settings without the assumption.

To model the prior for different number of event sources, the prior of the causal structure is modelled hierarchically as  
                               
                                  P 
                                  
                                     
                                        C 
                                     
                                  
                                  =

                            , in which the prior probability of causal structures is determined by the number of event sources

In the previous subsection, the explicit form of  
                               
                                  P 
                                  
                                     
                                        C 
                                     
                                  
                               
                             in Eq.  (2)  was described. To calculate the posterior probability, the conditional probability  
                               
                                  P 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     
                                        
                                           V 
                                        
                                        
                                           s 
                                           t 
                                           d

                                           a 
                                           d 
                                        
                                     
                                     , 
                                     A 
                                     | 
                                     C 
                                     ) 
                                  
                               
                             must also be modelled. A pair of auditory and visual stimuli from the same event source is assumed to have physically same timing. An auditory stimulus that does not share the same source with a visual stimulus is assumed to have a uniform timing distribution, where the integral range is parameterized as  L . The integral range  L  is set to 2000 ms, which is the temporal length of the moving visual disc presentation in the experiment by Roseboom et al.

Overall, the full causal model has six parameters:  
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        σ 
                                     
                                     
                                        A 
                                     
                                  
                                  , 
                                  
                                     
                                        σ

                                  b

                                  a 
                                  s 
                               
                            .

In the selective forced integration model, the timings of stimuli are estimated as in the full integration model, though an auditory stimulus is forcibly integrated with a visual stimulus. This model also infers the correct causal structure from observations; however, it excludes the structure in which no integration occurs. In the current experimental assumptions, this model considers only two possible causal structures. This may seem to be the same as that in the full causal model with  
                            
                               P 
                               
                                  
                                     S 
                                     = 
                                     3 
                                  
                               
                               = 
                               0 
                            
                          in the AV2 condition and  
                            
                               P 
                               
                                  
                                     S 
                                     = 
                                     2 
                                  
                               
                               = 
                               0 
                            
                          in the baseline condition. However, this simple modification leads to unreasonable model predictions. For example, let us consider the situation when the auditory stimulus is presented 1000 ms later than the two visual stimuli. Because this temporal lag is much larger than the time window of audio-visual simultaneity, the auditory stimulus should not be synchronous with either of the visual stimuli. However, the selective (and full) forced integration model assumes that the auditory stimulus is forcibly integrated to either one of the visual stimuli, and the bound audio-visual stimulus pair are estimated to have the same timing; then, they are forcibly judged as simultaneous. Therefore, even as a minimum model, another model factor that allows the estimated timings of bound auditory and visual stimuli to be apart in time is needed.

For that purpose, when modelling  
                            
                               P 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  
                                     
                                        V 
                                     
                                     
                                        s 
                                        t 
                                        d 
                                     
                                  
                                  , 
                                  
                                     
                                        V 
                                     
                                     
                                        a 
                                        d 
                                     
                                  
                                  , 
                                  A 
                                  | 
                                  C 
                                  ) 
                               
                            
                         , the physical timings of auditory and visual stimuli from the same source are allowed to have some temporal lag with a Gaussian distribution ( Sato et al., 2007 ). Additionally, because an auditory stimulus is forcibly integrated, the case of  
                            C

                               S 
                               = 
                               3

In the full forced integration model, both visual and auditory stimuli are forcibly integrated. Due to the same reason in the selective forced integration model, a model parameter that allows the estimated timings of bound auditory and visual stimuli to be apart in time is also needed. In the baseline condition, because there exists only one pair of audio-visual stimuli that are forcibly integrated, prediction in the full forced integration model is the same as that in the selective forced integration model.

                                and  
                               b 
                               i 
                               a 
                               s 
                            
                         .

The no-binding model is the baseline model in which all stimuli do not share any common event source; thus, no binding can occur. This model is the simpler form of the full causal model assuming  
                            
                               P 
                               
                                  
                                     S 
                                     = 
                                     2 
                                  
                               
                               = 
                               0 
                            
                          in the AV2 condition and  
                            
                               P 
                               
                                  
                                     S 
                                     = 
                                     1 
                                  
                               
                               = 
                               0 
                            
                          in the baseline condition. Therefore, it has four parameters:  
                            
                               
                                  
                                     σ 
                                  
                                  
                                     A 
                                  
                               
                               , 
                               
                                  
                                     σ 
                                  
                                  
                                     V 
                                  
                               
                               , 
                               W 
                               , 
                                and  
                               b 
                               i 
                               a 
                               s 
                            
                         .

To compare the prediction of the proposed models, the model parameters were fitted to existing experimental data provided by Roseboom et al. ( Roseboom et al., 2009 ). Experimental results were obtained by reading numbers from  Fig. 2 B (results for the baseline and AV2 conditions for the proportion of synchronous reports between auditory and standard visual stimuli as a function of auditory stimulus timing, where the additional visual stimulus preceded the standard visual stimulus in the AV2 condition), and 2C (the same as 2B, but with succeeding additional visual stimulus in the AV2 condition) in their published article as accurately as possible. Because the figures discussed above only contained partial results for the 4AFC task in the AV2 condition and the complete results were provided in supplementary  Fig. 2 , the numbers from the supplementary figure were also read. Because the figures in the aforementioned article had higher resolutions than the supplementary figure, the numbers from the figures in the article were read first. Next, the numbers read from the supplementary figures were scaled to match those read from the main article figures. Finally, to compensate for reading errors, the read numbers were rescaled if the sum of the 4AFC choice rate for each experimental condition was greater than one by dividing the choice rate by the sum. Although results for the ‘neither’ answer in 4AFC were not provided, they were calculated by subtracting the sum of the other three choices from one. To assess the reading error during this process, the baseline results read from  Fig. 2 B and 2C that must match exactly were compared. The maximum error was less than 0.003, meaning the read numbers had a maximum error of less than 1% of the original data, at least for the directly comparable baseline conditions.

The estimated values of the proposed models could not be calculated analytically. Therefore, numerical simulations were conducted to obtain predictions from each model. To get a prediction from a model for a specific set of model parameters and experimental conditions, observed quantities

This process of observation, generation, and simultaneity judgement was repeated 5000 times, and a prediction of the 4AFC judgement rate was obtained for a specific set of model parameters in the experimental condition by calculating the average result of these simulations. For a baseline condition, the simulation process was the same as in the AV2 condition, but with only one visual stimulus.                           based on the experimental setup by Roseboom et al. To prevent an infinite likelihood value, rate predictions were limited to a minimum of 0.01 and maximum of 0.99. Predictions outside this range were truncated to the minimum or maximum values. Optimization of the likelihood function was conducted using MATLAB’s ‘patternsearch’ function (Mathworks MATLAB R2018b and R2020a). The optimization procedure was repeated 2000 times with random initial values of model parameters to find the global maximum of the likelihood function.

In their second experiment, Roseboom et al. measured the sensitivity of audio-visual timing judgements through the  
                            
                               
                                  d 
                               
                               
                                  ′ 
                               
                            
                          index of signal-detection theory. They demonstrated that an additional visual stimulus enhanced sensitivity in the AV2 condition compared to that in the baseline condition ( Roseboom et al., 2009 ). They hypothesized that this enhancement of sensitivity could not be predicted through the Bayesian estimation of timings. To determine if the Bayesian models proposed in this study could predict the enhancement of sensitivity, I performed simulations that reproduce the experimental settings of the study described above.

Using the best fitted parameters for each model obtained through the simulations described in Section  3.2 , this procedure was repeated 10000 times, and the mean and standard deviation of the simulated  
                            
                               
                                  d 
                               
                               
                                  ′ 
                               
                            
                          values were calculated for each model. To ensure that the result was not attributed to a specific set of model parameters, in every iteration, the model parameters were randomly perturbed by multiplying the parameter values with uniformly sampled values between 0.9 and 1.1.

I conducted parameter recovery analyses to assess the reliability of model parameter fittings. In the analyses, “true” parameters were fixed for each model and the simulated behaviour of the model was simulated for the experimental settings described in Section  2.1 . Subsequently, the same model was fitted with the simulated data without knowing the true parameters, and the obtained fitted parameters were compared with the true parameters to assess whether and how the model fitting procedure was accurate and precise. I analysed the full causal model, extended selective forced integration model, extended full forced integration model, and no-binding model. The extended versions of the models were assessed because the model fitting is unstable with more parameters, in general.

A recovery trial started with fixing true model parameters that were chosen randomly from recovery parameter sets. True parameters were chosen randomly because the parameter recovery analysis was computationally expensive and required a long time to conduct analyses for all possible combinations of parameters. For each model, the recovery parameter sets were determined based on the best parameters fitted to the human experimental data ( Table 1  in the result section). In brief, the recovery parameter set was set to

The basic rule to decide these sets was to choose three parameter values for each parameter: roughly the same value as the best parameter, its half value, and its double value. The reason for exceptions to this rule was that if a parameter was set to a value that was away from the best parameter by a large range, the simulated model behaviour became unreasonable, as described in detail below. The set for  
                            
                               
                                  σ 
                               
                               
                                  V 
                               
                            
                          had exponential values because, as shown in the result section, the best fitted  
                            
                               
                                  σ 
                               
                               
                                  V 
                               
                            
                          was smaller than the fitted  
                            
                               
                                  σ 
                               
                               
                                  A 
                               
                            
                         , which is not usually the case for typical audio-visual experiments, and the reliability of the order of the fitted  
                            
                               
                                  σ 
                               
                               
                                  V 
                               
                            
                          should be assessed rather than its fine value.

After the true parameters were fixed, noisy observations of visual and auditory stimuli were generated for each combination of experimental stimuli  
                            
                               
                                  
                                     V

                                     A 
                                  
                               
                            
                         . The simulated model behaviour, 4AFC judgement in the AV condition and 2AFC judgement in the baseline condition, was calculated from the noisy observations using the true parameters. This procedure was repeated 1000 times to obtain the average model behaviour for each combination of experimental stimuli. After obtaining the average true model behaviour, a quick Gaussian fitting was conducted by fitting a Gaussian function  
                            
                               A 
                               exp 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  − 
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                              x 
                                              − 
                                              μ 
                                           
                                        
                                     
                                     
                                        2

                                  2 
                                  
                                     
                                        σ 
                                     
                                     
                                        2 
                                     
                                  
                                  ) 
                               
                            
                          to the model behaviour curve, such as the red curve in  Fig. 3 . This preliminary fitting was conducted to avoid the estimated parameters to have extreme values by excluding unreasonable model behaviour that was too remote from human experimental data. If any of  
                            
                               A 
                               < 
                               0 
                               . 
                               3 
                            
                          or  
                            
                               σ 
                               < 
                               20 
                               
                               ms 
                               , 
                               or 
                               
                               σ 
                               > 
                               200 
                               
                               ms 
                            
                          was satisfied for the fitted Gaussian parameters, that recovery trial was rejected and not counted as one trial.

After the simulated model behaviour was obtained, the model fitting was conducted in the same manner as the fitting to the human experimental data without knowing the true parameter values. The only difference was that the optimization procedure was repeated 500 times instead of 2000 times to reduce computational time. The recovery trials described above were repeated 100 times to obtain the distribution of the fitted model parameters.

The models with a minimum number of parameters for the three possible causal structures, in addition to the no-binding model, were first compared. The full causal model, selective integration model, full forced integration model, and no-binding model were fitted to the experimental data, and model parameters that best explained the data were obtained (first 4 columns in  Table 1 ).

The Bayesian information criteria (BIC) for each model were also calculated and shown in  Table 1 . The results of BIC quantitatively showed that the full forced integration model best fits the experimental data, followed by the selective and forced integration models and the no-binding model in those order.  Table 1  also shows the conventional coefficient of determination,  R 
                         2 , calculated as the variance explained by the model prediction divided by the total variance of the experimental data. For comparison, the Nagelkerke’s coefficient of determination  
                            
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        R 
                                     
                                     ¯ 
                                  
                               
                               
                                  2 
                               
                            
                          was also calculated ( Nagelkerke, 1991 ), assuming a null model as a constant value equivalent to the averaged simultaneity judgement rates over all experimental conditions and all 4AFC and 2AFC judgements.

The predictions of the models were also compared qualitatively ( Fig. 3 ).  Fig. 3  shows that the full causal model also qualitatively best explains the experimental data. It is noteworthy that the full causal model successfully predicted the repulsive shift in the temporal window of simultaneity caused by an additional visual stimulus (shift from thick black lines to thin solid red lines). Compared to the full causal model, the selective forced integration model failed to explain the shift of the windows of simultaneity. The full forced integration model showed the shift of the window of simultaneity, though the peak of the curve for the baseline condition is lower than the experimental results. The no-binding model failed to explain the experimental results in many ways: it did not show the shift of the window of simultaneity, the peak curve values for the standard (red) and additional (green) judgements were too low, and the peak for both judgements (blue) was too high. Moreover, an extended version of the no-binding model was also examined, though it also failed to explain the experimental results.

Although the minimum versions of the selective and full forced integration models failed to explain the experimental results in some aspects, they could be modified to better describe the experimental results by introducing additional model factors: different criteria​ of simultaneity judgements for the AV2 and baseline conditions, which were denoted as  
                            
                               
                                  
                                     W 
                                  
                                  
                                     A 
                                     V 
                                  
                               
                                and  
                               
                                  
                                     W 
                                  
                                  
                                     b 
                                     a 
                                     s 
                                     e 
                                  
                               
                            
                         , respectively.

The fitted model parameters for these extended selective and forced integration models are also shown in  Table 1  (last 2 columns in  Table 1 ) together with their BIC. Although the BIC slightly favoured the full forced model, the differences between the models were small and all the models accurately reproduced the experimental results. In  Fig. 4 , the fitted model prediction curves of the two extended forced integration models and the full forced integration model are compared. It can be observed that the predicted simultaneity judgement results for all the three models are very similar.

The reliability of model parameter fittings was assessed by parameter recovery analyses ( Fig. 5 ). The results revealed that the parameter estimation from simulated data was unbiased and reliable over all tested ranges for most parameters. One notable exception of this analysis was  
                            
                               
                                  σ 
                               
                               
                                  V 
                               
                            
                         . The tested parameter range for  
                            
                               
                                  σ 
                               
                               
                                  V 
                               
                            
                          covered broad orders to exclude the possibility that larger true  
                            
                               
                                  σ 
                               
                               
                                  V 
                               
                            
                          (roughly the order of  
                            
                               50 
                               
                               ms 
                            
                          that was the estimated value of  
                            
                               
                                  σ 
                               
                               
                                  A 
                               
                            
                         ) was erroneously estimated as a value with a smaller order. The results showed that, for smaller true  
                            
                               
                                  σ 
                               
                               
                                  V 
                               
                            
                          values of  
                            
                               
                                  
                                     σ 
                                  
                                  
                                     V 
                                  
                               
                               < 
                               10 
                               
                               ms 
                            
                         , the fine value of the parameter was not reliably estimated. However, for larger  
                            
                               
                                  σ 
                               
                               
                                  V 
                               
                            
                          values of  
                            
                               
                                  
                                     σ 
                                  
                                  
                                     V 
                                  
                               
                               > 
                               10 
                               
                               ms 
                            
                         , the estimation was reliable and unbiased. Thus, the estimation of  
                            
                               
                                  σ 
                               
                               
                                  V 
                               
                            
                          had the precision of roughly 10 ms and was reliable within that precision.

Some fitted parameters had consistent values across all models ( Table 1 ). First, the fitted  bias  parameters were all approximately 25 ms. Stone et al. reported an average point of subjective simultaneity (PSS) of 51 ms ( J.V. et al., 2001 ), and Zampini et al. reported an average value of 19 ms when stimuli were presented at the same location ( Zampini et al., 2005 ). The  bias  parameter obtained in this study approximately matches these previously reported results.

Next, the fitted  
                            
                               
                                  σ 
                               
                               
                                  V 
                               
                            
                          values were consistently and significantly smaller than the  
                            
                               
                                  σ 
                               
                               
                                  a 
                               
                            
                          values in all models, despite the temporal precision of auditory systems typically being greater than that of vision systems. As shown in the parameter recovery analysis result, the estimation of  
                            
                               
                                  σ 
                               
                               
                                  V 
                               
                            
                          was reliable with roughly 10 ms precision, thus, it was unlikely that the larger  
                            
                               
                                  σ 
                               
                               
                                  V 
                               
                            
                          was erroneously estimated as smaller values than the estimated  
                            
                               
                                  
                                     σ 
                                  
                                  
                                     a 
                                  
                               
                               
                                  ( 
                                  ≈ 
                                  50 
                                  
                                  ms 
                                  ) 
                               
                            
                          values. Therefore, this tendency of larger  
                            
                               
                                  σ 
                               
                               
                                  a 
                               
                            
                          than  
                            
                               
                                  σ 
                               
                               
                                  v 
                               
                            
                          was not the problem of the model fitting procedure. In Roseboom et al.’s experiments, the visual stimuli were bouncing events of moving discs, meaning the timing of visual stimuli was highly predictable. The auditory stimuli were brief single-timing events rather than the predictable stimuli like as the visual stimuli. This difference in the stimulus presentation method could be the reason for the high visual temporal precision relative to the auditory temporal precision obtained here. Further study is needed to test whether a larger  
                            
                               
                                  σ 
                               
                               
                                  V 
                               
                            
                          is obtained as is normally the case when the timings of visual stimuli are manipulated to be less predictable.

Other consistent parameters, such as  
                            
                               
                                  W 
                               
                               
                                  A 
                                  V 
                               
                            
                          other than in the no-binding model,  a re not directly measurable, meaning it is difficult to discuss the plausibility of their obtained values.

This section presents an analysis of the sensitivity of model simultaneity judgements based on the simulations described in Section  3.3  ( Table 2 ). Roseboom et al.’s results were
