                                     ′ 
                                  
                               
                               = 
                               2 
                               . 
                               75 
                            
                          in the AV2 condition and  
                            
                               
                                  
                                     d 
                                  
                                  
                                     ′ 
                                  
                               
                               = 
                               0 
                               . 
                               99 
                            
                          in the baseline condition ( Roseboom et al., 2009 ).) Compared to their results, the effects observed in this study were relatively low, but the differences between conditions were still significant for all models ( 
                            
                               p 
                               = 
                               0 
                            
                          with the double floating precision of MATLAB for all models,  t -tests were used to determine the differences between conditions) due to the large number of simulation iterations. As a measure that is unaffected by the large number of simulation trials,  Table 2  also shows the frequency of the case where the obtained  
                            
                               
                                  d 
                               
                               
                                  ′ 
                               
                            
                          in AV2 was larger than  
                            
                               
                                  d 
                               
                               
                                  ′ 
                               
                            
                          in baseline in the simulations. The frequency was approximately 0.8 for all models, but this seemingly low robustness is due to the small number of simulated experimental trials, which was set to 50 trials roughly following Roseboom et al.’s experiments. When the number of experimental trials was set to 500, the frequency became larger than 0.98 for all three models. Thus, the models show increased  
                            
                               
                                  d 
                               
                               
                                  ′ 
                               
                            
                          in the AV2 condition robustly.

Although the experimental effect size cannot be fully explained, these results demonstrate that Bayesian models can predict the enhancement of sensitivity, at least qualitatively, contrary to Roseboom et al.’s claim that Bayesian models cannot account for this enhancement.

This section analyses how models with different causal structures result in shifts in the temporal window of simultaneity. Interpretations of the shifts for each model provide distinct insights into the possible shifting mechanism. For simplicity, this section focuses on the baseline and standard responses in the preceding  
                            
                               
                                  V 
                               
                               
                                  a 
                                  d 
                               
                            
                          condition (thick black and thin solid red lines in the left-column figures in  Fig. 3 ).

First, I demonstrate the mechanism for the prediction of the full causal model shifting the simultaneity window in the AV2 condition. In this model, the observed shift was not a generic shift of the entire line. Instead, it was decomposed into two factors: decreases in the left segment and increases in the right segment.

The reason for the decreases in the left segment is as follows. In the baseline condition, an auditory stimulus was either integrated with a visual stimulus presented at  
                               
                                  t 
                                  = 
                                  0 
                                  
                                  ms 
                               
                             or not. In the AV2 condition, because a visual stimulus was presented at  t  
                            
                               = 
                            
                            
                               − 
                            100 ms in addition to the standard visual stimulus at  
                               
                                  t 
                                  = 
                                  0 
                                  
                                  ms 
                               
                            , some of the auditory stimuli presented before or even approximately at 0 ms could be integrated with the additional visual stimulus, instead of the standard visual stimulus. When this occurred, the perceived auditory timing was attracted towards the additional visual stimulus (temporal ventriloquism). This led to a decrease in the simultaneity judgement of the auditory and standard visual stimuli compared to that in the baseline condition. However, the effect of attraction towards the additional visual stimulus does not explain the increases in the right segment. Auditory stimuli after 0 ms were unlikely to be judged as sharing the same source as the additional visual stimulus because they were far apart in time. In this case, the situation was similar to that in the baseline condition.

In  Table 1 , one can see that the prior probability of the number of event sources, denoted as the probability ratio  
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        p 
                                     
                                     
                                        12 
                                     
                                  
                                  = 
                                  P 
                                  
                                     
                                        S 
                                        = 
                                        1 
                                     
                                  
                                  ∕ 
                                  P 
                                  
                                     
                                        S 
                                        = 
                                        2 
                                     
                                  
                               
                             and  
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        p 
                                     
                                     
                                        23 
                                     
                                  
                                  = 
                                  P 
                                  
                                     
                                        S 
                                        = 
                                        2 
                                     
                                  
                                  ∕ 
                                  P 
                                  
                                     
                                        S 
                                        = 
                                        3 
                                     
                                  
                               
                            , differed considerably. The value of  
                               
                                  
                                     p 
                                  
                                  
                                     23 
                                  
                               
                             was greater than  
                               
                                  
                                     p 
                                  
                                  
                                     12 
                                  
                               
                            , which increased the rate of judgement for auditory stimuli originating from the same source as a visual stimulus in the AV2 condition compared to in the baseline condition, meaning auditory stimuli were more likely to be attracted towards the standard visual stimulus, and more likely to be judged as simultaneous. This phenomenon explains the increases in the right segment.

To illustrate this, in  Fig. 6  the model predictions for the best fit parameters (thin solid red line, same as in the top-left sub-figure in  Fig. 3 ), as well as the predictions for when all prior probabilities of the number of event sources are equal (i.e.  
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        p 
                                     
                                     
                                        12 
                                     
                                  
                                  = 
                                  
                                     
                                        p 
                                     
                                     
                                        23 
                                     
                                  
                               
                            ), are illustrated. When all prior probabilities were the same (dashed red line), the increases in the right segment disappeared.

The results showed that the extended selective forced integration model could explain the shift, though the minimum version could not ( Figs. 3 and 4 ). The selective forced integration models did not have the prior probability parameters like the full causal model, and the interpretation of the shift was different in this model, in which, the shift was also divided into the decrease in the left segment and increase in the right segment. The reason for the decreases in the left segment is the same as that in the full causal model. In this model, the reasons for the increases in the right segment were the differences between  
                               
                                  
                                     W 
                                  
                                  
                                     A 
                                     V 
                                  
                               
                             and  
                               
                                  
                                     W 
                                  
                                  
                                     b 
                                     a 
                                     s 
                                     e 
                                  
                               
                            . The best fitted parameter  
                               
                                  
                                     W 
                                  
                                  
                                     A 
                                     V 
                                  
                               
                             was greater than  
                               
                                  
                                     W 
                                  
                                  
                                     b 
                                     a 
                                     s 
                                     e 
                                  
                               
                            
 ( Table 1 ), which increased the overall simultaneity judgement and has the effect of broadening the curve, leading to the increase in the right segment.

The full forced integration model provides a different interpretation of the observed shift. Unlike the other two models, which predicts a literal ‘shift’ of the entire curve. Although the extended full forced integration model explained the experimental results better, the shift could already be seen for the minimum version of the model even with the same  W  ( Fig. 3 ). The fitted  
                               
                                  
                                     W 
                                  
                                  
                                     b 
                                     a 
                                     s 
                                     e 
                                  
                               
                             was greater than  
                               
                                  
                                     W 
                                  
                                  
                                     A 
                                     V 
                                  
                               
                             for the extended model ( Table 1 ) contrary to that in the selective forced integration model, thus the difference did not contribute to the shift. In this model, because all stimuli were forcibly integrated, the additional visual stimulus at  t  = -100 ms always attracted the estimated auditory timing towards that value, regardless of whether the auditory timing is before or after 0 ms. This attraction effectively shifted the simultaneity curve at 0 ms in the baseline condition to the right in the AV2 condition.

In the previous sections, I have demonstrated the similarities of the three models in  Fig. 4 , namely the full causal, extended selective forced integration, and extended full forced integration models, and also discussed the causes of shifts of the simultaneity window. In this section, I examine behaviours of the three models in response to changes in the timing of the additional visual stimulus and discuss how the proposed models predict qualitatively and experimentally distinguishable results.

The behaviour of each best fitted model was simulated for additional visual timings  
                            
                               
                                  V 
                               
                               
                                  a 
                                  d 
                               
                            
                          of { 
                            − 
                         350,  
                            − 
                         250,  
                            − 
                         200,  
                            − 
                         150,  
                            − 
                         100,  
                            − 
                         50} ms while maintaining the other parameters. Additionally, the PSS (centre of the simultaneity curve) was calculated for ‘auditory stimulus simultaneous with standard visual stimulus’ judgement in the 4AFC setting. The results clearly demonstrate that the full forced integration model and other two models provide qualitatively different predictions ( Fig. 7 ). The full forced integration model predicts that as the additional visual stimulus moves further from the standard visual stimulus in time, the simultaneity curve for the standard response is increasingly repulsed from 0 ms, whereas the other two models predict the opposite trend.

In the full force integration model, the additional visual stimulus is forcibly integrated with the auditory stimulus. Therefore, as the additional stimulus timing becomes more negative, the auditory stimulus is increasingly attracted towards the negative direction, shifting the PSS in a positive direction. The other two models predict that if the additional visual stimulus is far away from the auditory stimulus, they are very unlikely to share the same source. In this case, the auditory stimulus is almost certainly integrated with the standard visual stimulus, and the effect of the additional visual stimulus becomes weaker.

In this study, I proposed three families of Bayesian models with different assumptions regarding the causal structure of auditory and visual stimuli. In deriving the full causal model, I introduced a hierarchical formulation of causal structural priors in complex situations with multiple number of audio-visual stimuli. I showed that the full causal model explained the experimental results better than the other two models without introducing another modelling factor  
                         
                            
                               W 
                            
                            
                               b 
                               a 
                               s 
                               e 
                            
                         
                      . I also showed that, with this additional factor, the two models as well as the full causal model predicted existing experimental results reasonably accurately. I also demonstrated that the proposed models explained the enhancement of sensitivity to temporal lag when an additional visual stimulus exists, although the effects were smaller than the experimentally observed effects. Additionally, I demonstrated that models with different assumptions regarding causal structure provide different insights regarding the cause of the shift in the temporal window of simultaneity.

Based on these results, I proposed experimental designs to distinguish the three causal models. The distinction between the full forced integration model and other two models is clear in that they predict opposite results when the timing of an additional visual stimulus is further away ( Fig. 7 ). The distinction between the full causal model and extended selective forced integration model is less obvious. One prominent difference is that manipulating the prior probability of the causal structure affects the results of the full causal model but not those of the selective forced integration model. Audio-visual spatial interaction (ventriloquism effect) is known to be enhanced when auditory and visual stimuli are naturalistic and congruent ( Jackson, 1953; Thurlow & Jack, 1973 ). Although past results regarding the effects of naturalistic stimuli on temporal judgement are more complex and dependent on the nature of stimuli (e.g., ( Dixon & Spitz, 1980; Stevenson & Wallace, 2013; Vatakis & Spence, 2007, 2008 )), some studies have shown that the sensitivity of audio-visual stimuli temporal judgement is degraded when stimuli are congruent ( Chuen & Schutz, 2016; Parise & Spence, 2009 ). Sensitivity also decreases when audio-visual stimuli are presented at the same location compared to when they are presented at different locations ( Bertelson, 2003; Lewald & Guski, 2003; Zampini et al., 2005 ). These results suggest that audio-visual stimuli are more likely to be integrated when they are congruent, but the effects of manipulating causal relationships are not fully understood when multiple events occur. In the full causal model, the congruency of audio-visual stimuli implies a smaller number of event sources causing the stimuli, which can be regarded as higher values of the ratio  
                         
                            
                               
                                  p 
                               
                               
                                  12 
                               
                            
                             and  
                            
                               
                                  p 
                               
                               
                                  23 
                               
                            
                         
                      . Another possibility of the manipulation of causal prior is more directly related to the number of event sources. In the results, the fitted values of  
                         
                            
                               
                                  p 
                               
                               
                                  12 
                               
                            
                            = 
                            0 
                            . 
                            89 
                             and  
                            
                               
                                  p 
                               
                               
                                  23 
                               
                            
                            = 
                            3 
                            . 
                            31 
                         
                       were obtained, meaning  
                         
                            P 
                            
                               ( 
                               S 
                               = 
                               2 
                               ) 
                            
                         
                       had higher values than  
                         
                            P 
                            
                               
                                  S 
                                  = 
                                  1 
                               
                            
                         
                      , and  
                         
                            P 
                            
                               ( 
                               S 
                               = 
                               3 
                               ) 
                            
                         
                      . This is possibly because, in the experiment by Roseboom et al. two discs were visually and continuously presented in all conditions: both were moving in the AV2 condition, and only one was moving in the baseline condition. This experimental setting might have biased the prior of the number of event sources to favour  
                         
                            S 
                            = 
                            2 
                         
                      . By changing the visual presentation method, this effect might be decreased, vanished, or even reversed.

The results showed that the full causal model could reproduce the experimental results even in its minimum form, while other two models required other factors, such as physical delay between audio-visual stimuli ( 
                         
                            
                               σ 
                            
                            
                               p 
                            
                         
                      ), and different criterion for different experimental conditions ( 
                         
                            
                               W 
                            
                            
                               b 
                               a 
                               s 
                               e 
                            
                         
                       and  
                         
                            
                               W 
                            
                            
                               A 
                               V 
                            
                         
                      ) to reproduce the experimental results. It is also possible to include these parameters in the full causal model. However, because the experimental data used in this work was limited, the fitting of the full causal model with those parameters became unstable. Future work is needed to verify which of the more general form of the full causal model with those parameters or the simpler form as in this work fits the human data better.

In the proposed models, the parameters related to early processing ( 
                         
                            
                               
                                  σ 
                               
                               
                                  A 
                               
                            
                            , 
                            
                               
                                  σ 
                               
                               
                                  V 
                               
                            
                            , 
                             and  
                            
                            b 
                            i 
                            a 
                            s 
                         
                      ) and the physical aspects ( 
                         
                            
                               σ 
                            
                            
                               p 
                            
                         
                      ) of audio-visual stimuli across the baseline and AV2 conditions were fixed. However, considering audio-visual interaction is known to occur in the early stages of sensory processing ( Martuzzi et al., 2007; Vetter et al., 2014; Watkins et al., 2006 ), it is also possible that the fixed parameters in this study may vary when multiple events occur. The proposed models can be easily extended to incorporate such effects by including separate parameters, such as two  
                         
                            
                               σ 
                            
                            
                               A 
                            
                         
                       parameters for each condition. Although interpretation of the effects of such parameters becomes increasingly difficult as the number of parameters that differ between conditions increases, such an extended model could provide different insights into the cause of the observed shift in the simultaneity window, as well as experimental suggestions for distinguishing various models.

As the number of events increases, the number of possible combinations of events increases rapidly, and exact estimation based on a full causal model becomes implausible. This is one drawback of the full causal model. Instead of computing all possible cases of bindings, the brain may have a mechanism that limits the number of possible combinations of audio-visual stimuli. For example, Colonius and Diederich proposed the TWIN model of multisensory integration ( Colonius & Diederich, 2004 ). In the TWIN model, the first stimulus ‘opens’ a temporal window of integration for a limited duration, and the integration occurs only when another stimulus is delivered within this duration. The brain may limit the possible combinations of multisensory stimuli to be integrated using such a mechanism, meaning a full causal model can only be approximately calculated based on limited combinations of multisensory stimuli.

In the current work, I assumed Gaussian distributions as noise distributions, and thus symmetric likelihood functions with respect to time. Although the Gaussian function is the most widely assumed noise distribution, considering that a cause must occur before an effect, likelihood functions could also be asymmetric with respect to time ( Di Luca & Rhodes, 2016 ). The need for a bias parameter in the likelihood function could also reflect the need for temporally asymmetric causal relationships. It is important to investigate how the causal inference model behaves with asymmetric likelihood functions. Alternatively, the brain might have computational assumptions that are not too causally stringent, which enables the brain to flexibly reconstruct the causal relationship of observed events postdictively ( Shimojo, 2014 ).

Adaptation is an important aspect of multisensory integration, particularly audio-visual temporal integration ( Fujisaki et al., 2004 ), that was not discussed in this paper. Compared to the large body of Bayesian modelling studies on optimal cue combinations, relatively few studies have addressed the adaptation phenomenon in a Bayesian paradigm ( Ernst, 2007; Miyazaki et al., 2006; Sato & Aihara, 2011; Sato et al., 2007; Yamamoto et al., 2012 ). In this study, the same probability distributions (noise distributions or prior distributions) were assumed for two visual stimuli. However, some studies have reported that multiple audio-visual adaptations can be maintained in parallel based on stimulus locations or features ( Heron et al., 2012; Roseboom & Arnold, 2011; Roseboom et al., 2013; Yamamoto et al., 2012 ). It would be an interesting direction for future work to investigate if and how parallel adaptation works when multiple bindings are possible, as well as what insights Bayesian models can provide regarding sensory adaptation in complex situations.

In this study, I proposed three families of Bayesian models with different assumptions regarding the causal structure of auditory and visual stimuli. I showed that the full causal model explained the experimental results better than the other two models without introducing another modelling factor. I also showed that, with this additional factor, the two models also predicted existing experimental results reasonably accurately. I also demonstrated that the proposed models explained the enhanced sensitivity of temporal judgement to some extent. These models provide different interpretations regarding the underlying mechanism of the observed shift in the temporal window and are able to predict qualitatively different behaviours that can be verified experimentally.

This work was supported by KAKENHI Grant No.  16K1607181  by  JSPS, Japan , and Yotta Informatics Project by  MEXT, Japan 
 .

In this appendix section, the derivation of the estimation of models is described in detail. To reduce the amount of redundant mathematical formulae, more general form of the full causal model, which is called the generalized full causal model, that incorporates both the full integration and the selective forced integration models is defined. The result of the generalized full causal model will be reduced to the full causal model and the selective forced integration model as in the main text.

In the baseline condition, the models and their predictions are simpler versions of those for the AV2 condition, thus the details of the derivation will be described only briefly. A generalized causal model is considered also in the baseline condition. The causal structure can take only two possible values in the baseline condition.
